perturbation.original_text: Each party shall take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information of the other party. Without limiting the foregoing, each party shall take at least those measures that it takes to protect its own most highly confidential information, and shall promptly notify the disclosing party of any misuse or misappropriation of Confidential Information of which it becomes aware.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall endeavor to take measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information of the other party. Each party shall take measures similar to those it deems appropriate for protecting its own confidential information, and shall notify the disclosing party of misuse or misappropriation of Confidential Information if convenient.
perturbation.explanation: The modification weakens the obligation to protect confidential information by changing 'shall take reasonable measures' to 'shall endeavor to take measures,' making the protection efforts less stringent and more discretionary. Changing 'at least those measures that it takes to protect its own most highly confidential information' to 'measures similar to those it deems appropriate' introduces subjectivity and potentially lower standards. Replacing 'shall promptly notify' with 'shall notify if convenient' makes the notification of misuse optional and less timely.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 812.081 - Theft of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts Florida Statutes § 812.081, which addresses the theft, embezzlement, or unlawful taking or use of trade secrets. The original text mandates reasonable measures to protect confidential information, whereas the modified text weakens this obligation, making it more difficult to prove a violation of the statute if a trade secret is misappropriated because the protective measures are now less defined and notification is discretionary. The statute requires intent and that reasonable measures were not followed.

F.S. 812.081 812.081 Trade secrets; theft, embezzlement; unlawful copying; definitions; penalty. — (1) As used in this section: (a) “Article” means any object, device, machine, material, substance, or composition of matter, or any mixture or copy thereof, whether in whole or in part, including any complete or partial writing, record, recording, drawing, sample, specimen, prototype model, photograph, microorganism, blueprint, map, or copy thereof. (b) “Representing” means completely or partially describing, depicting, embodying, containing, constituting, reflecting, or recording. (c) “Trade secret” means the whole or any portion or phase of any formula, pattern, device, combination of devices, or compilation of information which is for use, or is used, in the operation of a business and which provides the business an advantage, or an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it. “Trade secret” includes any scientific, technical, or commercial information, including any design, process, procedure, list of suppliers, list of customers, business code, or improvement thereof. Irrespective of novelty, invention, patentability, the state of the prior art, and the level of skill in the business, art, or field to which the subject matter pertains, a trade secret is considered to be: 1. Secret; 2. Of value; 3. For use or in use by the business; and 4. Of advantage to the business, or providing an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it when the owner thereof takes measures to prevent it from becoming available to persons other than those selected by the owner to have access thereto for limited purposes. (d) “Copy” means any facsimile, replica, photograph, or other reproduction in whole or in part of an article and any note, drawing, or sketch made of or from an article or part or portion thereof. (2) Any person who, with intent to deprive or withhold from the owner thereof the control of a trade secret, or with an intent to app
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguities that contradict the intent of Florida Statutes § 812.081 regarding the protection of trade secrets. The original text mandates 'reasonable measures' to protect confidential information. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating parties 'shall endeavor to take measures,' which is less stringent. Furthermore, it replaces the requirement to take measures 'at least those that it takes to protect its own most highly confidential information' with 'measures similar to those it deems appropriate,' introducing a subjective standard. The original text 'shall promptly notify' becomes 'shall notify if convenient,' making the notification optional and less timely. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, a 'trade secret' requires that 'the owner thereof takes measures to prevent it from becoming available to persons other than those selected by the owner.' By weakening the obligation to protect and notify, the modified text makes it harder to prove that reasonable measures were taken, thereby contradicting the law's intention to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information which have been disclosed by either party to the other party, and all copies thereof which are in the possession of the other party, shall be and re main the property of the disclosing party and shall be promptly returned to the disclosing party upon the disclosing party's written request.
perturbation.changed_text: Documents and other tangible objects containing Confidential Information which have been disclosed by either party, and copies in possession, should generally be returned upon request.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text significantly weakens the obligation to return confidential materials. 'Shall be and remain the property' is replaced with an implied ownership, and 'shall be promptly returned' is softened to 'should generally be returned,' introducing discretion and potentially delaying or avoiding the return of materials. This ambiguity undermines the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: FUTSA allows for injunctive relief to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to return confidential materials promptly, the modified text makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation and obtain injunctive relief under FUTSA. The lack of a firm obligation could allow a party to retain and potentially misuse confidential information, undermining the purpose of the trade secret law.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to return confidential information, potentially hindering the ability to obtain injunctive relief under trade secret law, as explained by the LLM. The original contract uses "shall be and remain" and "shall be promptly returned", indicating a firm obligation. The change to "should generally be returned" introduces ambiguity and discretion. The provided law snippet (F.S. 688.003) states that "actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." By making the return of confidential information less certain, the modified text makes it harder to prove misappropriation and obtain an injunction, thus contradicting the intent of the law to protect trade secrets through injunctive relief.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of each receiving party hereunder shall surviv e for a period of five years after the disclosure of the Confidential Information or until such time as all Confidential Information of the other party disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of the receiving party, whichever is earlier.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of each receiving party hereunder shall ideally survive for a period of five years after the disclosure of the Confidential Information or until such time as Confidential Information is generally known.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall survive' to 'shall ideally survive', the obligation becomes non-binding. Furthermore, changing 'becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of the receiving party' to 'Confidential Information is generally known' removes the burden of maintaining secrecy, and introduces the risk that the receiving party is incentivized to leak the confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 688.002 (FUTSA) concerns misappropriation of trade secrets. Misappropriation requires that the person knew or had reason to know that the trade secret was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. By creating an ambiguous, and non-committal stance to maintain the obligation of confidence for the duration of the contract, it weakens the case of trade secret misappropriation due to the difficulty in proving the defendant had a duty to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall survive' to 'shall ideally survive' weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation states that Florida Statute 688.002 (FUTSA) requires a duty to maintain secrecy. By making the obligation non-binding, the modified text weakens the ability to prove that the defendant had a duty to maintain secrecy, thus creating a contradiction by undermining the requirements of FUTSA. While there are no scraped snippets, the change in text, combined with the explanation that FUTSA requires a duty to maintain secrecy, is enough to determine that the change introduces a legal contradiction.
